segments.cost: $15-$25 first class; $8-$12 emigrant class (1880s). The Northern Pacific offered heavily discounted 'land-seeker' fares — sometimes as low as $5 from St. Paul to the Dakota Territory — to lure potential homesteaders and land buyers.
segments.conditions: The line ran northwest from the Twin Cities across the Minnesota prairie and into the Dakota Territory — flat wheat country stretching to the horizon in every direction. Emigrant cars carried Scandinavian and German-Russian families with their belongings packed in wooden trunks and bundles tied with rope. Children slept on the floor between the benches. At Fargo, the Red River of the North marked the boundary between Minnesota and Dakota Territory, and beyond it the bonanza wheat farms of the Red River Valley spread out — operations so large that a single farm might encompass 30,000 acres worked by gangs of hired labor.

segments.cost: included in through ticket; land prices along this stretch: $2.50-$5.00 per acre for Northern Pacific grant lands
segments.conditions: West of Bismarck, the line crossed the Missouri River on a bridge that had delayed completion for years (Jay Cooke's bankruptcy in the Panic of 1873 halted construction at Bismarck for six years). The track crossed the badlands of western Dakota and the high plains of eastern Montana — country where winter temperatures dropped to forty below zero and summer heat baked the alkaline soil to dust. Cattle ranchers and sheep herders used the railroad to ship livestock east; homesteaders rode it west to claim 160-acre parcels of land the railroad's agents had promised would grow wheat as tall as a man's waist.

segments.travel_time: 24-30 hours (1880s-1890s); total St. Paul to Tacoma: approximately 3 days
segments.cost: Through ticket St. Paul to Tacoma: $50-$75 first class; $25-$40 emigrant class (1880s). The Northern Pacific's European agents sold package deals: steamship ticket from Scandinavia or Germany plus emigrant car to the Pacific Northwest.
segments.conditions: The line climbed over the Continental Divide at Mullan Pass (5,550 feet) west of Helena, descended through the Bitterroot Mountains and across the Idaho panhandle, then followed the Columbia River gorge and crossed the Cascades through Stampede Tunnel (completed 1888, nearly two miles long and so poorly ventilated that locomotive crews sometimes passed out from coal smoke). The final descent into Puget Sound brought passengers to Tacoma — the Northern Pacific's chosen terminus, selected over Seattle in a decision that fueled a rivalry lasting decades.

who_used_it: Scandinavian immigrants (Norwegian, Swedish, Finnish, Danish) recruited by Northern Pacific agents stationed in Christiania (Oslo), Stockholm, and Helsinki. German-Russian settlers from the Volga and Black Sea colonies who homesteaded in the Dakota Territory. Irish and Chinese laborers who built the western sections. Lumber workers and mining families headed for Montana copper towns (Butte, Anaconda) and Pacific Northwest timber camps. The NP's aggressive European recruitment campaign — pamphlets in seven languages promising free land and mild climate — brought tens of thousands of Scandinavian and German families to the northern Great Plains between 1880 and 1910.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} left {origin} aboard the Northern Pacific, riding the second transcontinental west through country so flat and cold and empty that the railroad's own pamphlets had lied about it — but the land was real, 160 free acres of it under the Homestead Act, waiting at {destination} for anyone stubborn enough to survive the first winter.
source_references: Louis Tuck Renz, 'The History of the Northern Pacific Railroad' (Ye Galleon Press, 1980)
Richard White, 'Railroaded: The Transcontinentals and the Making of Modern America' (W. W. Norton, 2011)
W. Thomas White, 'A History of Railroad Workers in the Pacific Northwest, 1883-1934' (PhD dissertation, University of Washington, 1981)
Carlton C. Qualey, 'Norwegian Settlement in the United States' (Norwegian-American Historical Association, 1938)
